steps: Deviations from mean for X: −2, −2, −1, −1, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2. Deviations from mean for Y: −2, −2, −1, −1, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2.
Standard deviation depends only on how far each value strays from the mean, not on the values themselves. Both sets have identical deviation patterns, so their standard deviations are equal: $q = s$.
That matches choice C — C is correct.

choices.elimination:
• This requires X to be less spread out than Y.
  • Both sets have identical deviations from their respective means (−2, −2, −1, −1, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2), so their standard deviations are equal, not unequal.
  • Eliminated because q is not less than s.

• This requires X to be more spread out than Y.
  • The deviation patterns are identical for both sets, making q = s, not q > s.
  • Eliminated for the same reason as A.

• Standard deviation measures spread around the mean using squared deviations.
  • X has deviations −2, −2, −1, −1, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2 from mean 15; Y has the exact same deviations from mean 4.
  • Because the deviation patterns are identical, $q = s$. That matches choice C — C is correct.

choices.text: The relationship between $q$ and $s$ cannot be determined.
choices.elimination:
• The relationship can actually be determined by computing the deviations from the mean for each set.
  • Both sets yield identical deviation patterns, so q = s is determinable.
  • Eliminated because a definite relationship exists.
